plainMeaning: Because of these and other such allowed reflexes, may my standing-still meditation stay whole and faultless.
recitation: evamāiehiṁ āgārehiṁ, abhaggo, avirāhio, hujja me kāussaggo.

wordByWord.gloss: by these-and-such, by (exceptions) of this kind and the like
wordByWord.etymology: evam ('thus, such') + āi (Skt ādi, 'and so forth, et cetera') + instrumental plural ending -ehiṁ; an adjective agreeing with āgārehiṁ
wordByWord.notes: The pivotal word: it does not name a new exception but gathers the foregoing list ('and others of the same sort'), leaving the āgāra-set open-ended.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt ākāra/āgāra in the technical vow-sense 'reservation'; instrumental plural, 'by means of the āgāras'
wordByWord.notes: Instrumental of cause: it is by/through these allowances that the kāyotsarga is kept intact.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt a- (negation) + bhagna ('broken', from √bhañj 'to break'); Prakrit bhagga → abhagga, nominative masc. sing. -o
wordByWord.notes: Predicate adjective of kāussaggo: not snapped or interrupted.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt a- + virādhita ('transgressed, injured', from vi-√rādh); Prakrit virāhia → avirāhia, nominative masc. sing.
wordByWord.notes: Pairs with abhaggo: abhagga negates external interruption, avirāhia negates inner fault/transgression.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit optative/benedictive of √hū/bhū ('to be'); = Skt bhavet / bhūyāt, 'may it be'
wordByWord.notes: The single optative governing the verse — a wish/resolve, not a statement of fact.

wordByWord.etymology: enclitic genitive-dative of the first person (Skt me); 'of me, mine'
wordByWord.notes: Marks the kāyotsarga as the reciter's own undertaking.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit kāussagga < Skt kāya-utsarga ('body' + 'releasing, laying down'); nominative masc. sing. -o, the grammatical subject
wordByWord.notes: Subject of hujja: 'may my kāyotsarga be (unbroken, unviolated).'

literalTranslation: By these and similar exceptions, unbroken, unviolated, may my kāyotsarga be.
idiomaticTranslation: Allowing for these and other such involuntary acts, may my kāyotsarga remain unbroken and unviolated.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This is the hinge of the formula: the verse stops listing and starts resolving. After the gross reflexes of verse 1 and the three subtle saṁcāla of verse 2, the practitioner does not attempt an exhaustive catalogue. The single word evamāiehiṁ — 'by these and the like' — deliberately leaves the set open: the ādi ('et cetera') signals that any further unavoidable, unwilled bodily act falls under the same permission. This is precisely why the wider tradition can speak of sixteen āgāra without contradicting the text — the verse itself invites such extension while naming no fixed number. The two predicate adjectives are carefully complementary, not redundant. Abhaggo ('unbroken') guards the kāyotsarga from the outside: it must not be cut short or snapped by a willed movement. Avirāhio ('unviolated') guards it from the inside: it must not be inwardly spoiled by transgression or careless fault even if outwardly maintained. Together they define a practice whole in both form and intention. The grammar reinforces the shift in mood: the whole verse hangs on one optative, hujja ('may it be'), turning the foregoing description of allowances into a personal vow — me, 'mine.' The reciter is no longer cataloguing what the body may do; he is binding himself to what the meditation must remain. Thus verse 3 converts a permission-list into a standard of success, setting up the temporal clause (jāva…tāva) that verses 4–5 will use to fix exactly when this 'unbroken, unviolated' state begins and ends.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse encodes the Jain logic of vow and intention (bhāva). A breach of conduct requires willed agency; an āgāra simply removes involuntary, mindless bodily events from the scope of the vow so they cannot count as transgression. By pairing abhaggo with avirāhio, the verse distinguishes the outer integrity of an observance from its inner purity — a recurring Jain concern that mere external performance (dravya) is empty without right intention (bhāva). The open-ended evamāiehiṁ also reflects the tradition's realism about embodiment: stillness is an aspiration the living body can only approximate, so the vow is framed to remain honest and keepable rather than impossibly absolute.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: When a reciter stands in kāyotsarga, this verse tells him what 'success' actually means: not a statue-like perfection, but a meditation kept whole (abhagga) and free of willed lapse (avirāhia), while natural reflexes are simply let go. Practically, it frees the mind from anxiety over a cough or a sway — those are covered — and redirects attention to the one thing that can break the practice: a deliberate movement or a wandering, faulting intention. The optative 'may it be' is meant to be felt as a personal resolve at the threshold of the silent meditation.

sources.consultedFor: cross-checking the standard rendering of the Annattha verse's āgāra and the abhagga/avirāhia pair

notes: Reading and orthography honored verbatim from the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipaksha) book. evamāiehiṁ is parsed evam+ādi+(instr. pl.), 'by these and the like'; an online auto-gloss misreads -āi- as 'by me' (confusing it with the genitive me later in the verse) — that reading is rejected as it contradicts the in-corpus gloss and the ādi morpheme. abhaggo and avirāhio are taken as complementary (outer 'unbroken' vs. inner 'unviolated'); some translators treat them as near-synonyms ('undisturbed/uninterrupted'), a defensible but flatter reading. The open evamāiehiṁ is the textual basis for the tradition-dependent expansion to sixteen āgāra, whose exact membership the verse does not enumerate.
